title: Catechist Guide — Human Work
cic: CCC 2427-2442, Laborem Exercens, Compendium of the Social Doctrine of the Church
html:
Catechist Guide

For preparing and leading the class. For the exclusive use of the catechist.

Lesson Summary

Estimated duration: 60 minutes

Central theme: Human work as participation in God's creative work, as an expression of the dignity of the person, and as a path to holiness for the Christian. The social doctrine of the Church on work: from St. Joseph the Worker to St. Josemaría Escrivá.

Objectives:

  • Understand that work is not a punishment, but participation in God's creative work (Gen 1:28; CCC 2427).
  • Discover St. Joseph the Worker as the model of sanctified, silent labor.
  • Distinguish between the objective and subjective dimensions of work (the primacy of the person over production).
  • Recognize the just wage and dignified conditions as demands of justice, not charity (CCC 2434).
  • Identify and reject unjust labor practices (modern slavery, exploitation, child labor).
  • Value Sunday rest as an act of resistance against the "worship of money."
  • Learn the spirituality of work: sanctifying work, being sanctified in work, sanctifying through work.

Key verse: "Whatever you do, work at it with all your heart, as working for the Lord, not for human masters" (Col 3:23)

Catechism references: CCC 2427-2442, 2184-2188, 2401-2418

Suggested Class Structure

Section Duration What the catechist does
1. Big Question (St. Joseph and the Nazareth workshop) 8 min Ask: "Is work a curse or a blessing?" Tell the story of Jesus' hidden life in Nazareth. Why did God choose to work 30 years before preaching 3?
2. Work as participation in creation (Gen 1-2) 8 min Explain that work existed in Eden, before sin. God "worked" six days. Work is an original vocation, not a punishment.
3. Objective vs. subjective dimension of work 8 min Teach the distinction from Laborem Exercens. Man > things. Being > having. Contrast Sisyphus (Greek myth) with Col 3:23.
4. Justice at work: wages, conditions, 7th Commandment 10 min Just wage as a demand of justice. Condemned practices (slavery, child exploitation, fraud). Zacchaeus as model of restitution (Lk 19).
5. Sunday rest: man is not made for work 8 min CCC 2172, 2184-2188. Sunday as "protest." Why is resting holy? Connection to the Sunday Eucharist.
6. Spirituality of work (St. Josemaría) 8 min Three dimensions: sanctifying work, being sanctified in work, sanctifying others. Work as prayer. Every task done with love has eternal value.
7. Closing: Key Idea + Prayer 10 min Read Key Idea. Reflect together. Pray the closing prayer. Hand out workbook.

Notes for the Catechist

The Nazareth workshop as a starting point

Many Christians separate "spiritual" life from "working" life. Mass on Sundays and during the week "the real world." The story of the Nazareth workshop destroys that separation. Jesus spent 30 years — 90% of his earthly life — working with his hands, and he sanctified every board he cut, every customer he served. Teach your students to identify where they have placed that false separation between "sacred" and "profane." Ask: "If Jesus worked in a workshop, can our work be something 'profane' that doesn't interest God?"

Work before sin (Gen 2:15)

This point is crucial because it dismantles the "work = necessary evil" mindset. Read Gen 2:15 aloud: "The LORD God took the man and put him in the Garden of Eden to work it and take care of it." Work — cultivating, caring — is a vocation of paradise. What sin introduced was fatigue, frustration (Gen 3:17-19), not work itself. This changes everything: our work is not a wound to endure; it is a vocation to redeem.

The subjective dimension: the worker matters more than the product

St. John Paul II was prophetic in insisting on this in 1981. In the age of artificial intelligence, automation, and the gig economy, this teaching is more urgent than ever. Ask: "When Uber treats you like an algorithm, when the company sees you as a number, when your boss never asks how you are — what does the Church say about that?" The answer: the Church says that it is anti-Christian. The person is not a resource. The person is a child of God.

The Seventh Commandment and property

The Catholic teaching on private property and the universal destination of goods is one of the most misunderstood. Neither wild capitalism ("my money is mine and I do what I want") nor Marxist collectivism ("the state owns everything"). The Catholic position: private property is legitimate and necessary, but it is subordinated to the universal destination of goods. Every owner is a steward, not an absolute owner. You can use the parable of the talents (Mt 25:14-30) or the rich fool (Lk 12:13-21) as illustrations.

Zacchaeus and restitution

Zacchaeus' conversion (Lk 19:1-10) is one of the most powerful stories about economic justice. Zacchaeus was a chief tax collector, wealthy, and had probably been stealing for years. When Jesus enters his house, Zacchaeus does not limit himself to weeping: he restores. And he doesn't restore the minimum (the Law of Moses required the value plus 20% in certain cases — Lev 5:16; Num 5:7), but fourfold. Zacchaeus' generosity proves the authenticity of his conversion. Ask your students: "How do you prove that your conversion is authentic? With words or with deeds?"

Rest as resistance to the idol of money

The Catechism says that Sunday is a "day of protest against the servitude of work and the worship of money" (CCC 2172). This is countercultural. In a world where shops open 24/7, where email pursues us on weekends, where "being busy" has become a status symbol — resting on Sunday is an act of faith. It tells our bosses, our colleagues, and ourselves: "I am not my productivity. I am a child of God."

St. Josemaría and the spirituality of work

St. Josemaría Escrivá (1902-1975) was canonized in 2002. His central message is that holiness is not only for priests and religious: every baptized person, in their ordinary work, is called to holiness. The three dimensions are easy to remember: (1) do the work well, (2) grow personally while working, (3) help others draw closer to God through their example. These are not "three different things"; they are three aspects of one and the same reality. Ask: "Have you ever known a Christian whose simple way of working drew you closer to God? Could you be that person?"

Adaptation by Age

🌱 Seed (children 7-12)

Use the story of the Nazareth workshop. Tell them that Jesus, as a child, helped St. Joseph sand wood, measure boards, sweep the workshop. Activity: each child draws Jesus and Joseph working together. Ask: "What work do you do at home? Do you help your parents? Can you offer that work to God?" Teach them a short prayer: "Jesus, I offer you this work. Help me to do it well, like you."

🌿 Shoots (youth 13-17)

Connect with their reality: study is their main "work." Many young people see study as an imposed burden, not as a vocation. Ask: "Why do you study? For good grades? To get into university? To earn money someday?" Help them see that studying can also be "for the Lord" (Col 3:23). Discuss the pressure of social media and the "productivity" displayed there: is that work or appearance? Christian work doesn't need likes.

🪴 Root (adults new to the faith)

Many adults have been wounded by the working world: unjust dismissals, abusive bosses, miserable wages. The social doctrine of the Church is medicine for those wounds. It is not "Christian resignation" (passively enduring everything). It is justice with hope: the Church acknowledges the harm, denounces it, and proposes paths of dignity. Some may need to reconcile with the Seventh Commandment — tax fraud, unworked hours, small normalized thefts. Propose the sacrament of Reconciliation without fear.

🌳 Tree (mature adults)

Deepen into Laborem Exercens and its historical context: the encyclical was written when communism still ruled half of Europe and Western capitalism was beginning its neoliberal turn. St. John Paul II rejects both ideologies. You can also discuss texts of the Church Fathers: St. John Chrysostom ("What the rich have in excess belongs to the poor"), St. Basil, St. Ambrose on property as stewardship. For those working in finance or business: recommend reading the parable of the talents (Mt 25) and discerning Christian fiduciary responsibility.

Supplementary Resources

  • YouCat: Questions 328-331 ("Work," "Property and the Economy")
  • Compendium of the Catechism: Questions 512-520
  • Laborem Exercens (St. John Paul II, 1981) — Encyclical on human work
  • Rerum Novarum (Leo XIII, 1891) — The foundational encyclical of modern social doctrine
  • Compendium of the Social Doctrine of the Church — Chapters VI and VII (human work and economic life)
  • St. Josemaría Escrivá: Christ Is Passing By (Homily "Passionately Loving the World"), Furrow, The Way
  • Video: "St. Joseph the Worker — The Value of Work" on Catholic Link YouTube
  • Film: Romero (1989) — The life of St. Óscar Romero and his commitment to social justice
  • Book: "Working Well, Working for Love" — Anthology of St. Josemaría's texts on work

Difficult Questions

"Isn't the social doctrine of the Church a disguised form of socialism?"

No. Catholic social doctrine defends both private property and social justice, and rejects both collectivism (which denies personal freedom) and radical economic liberalism (which denies social responsibility). Leo XIII wrote Rerum Novarum to oppose socialism, but also to denounce the abuses of industrial capitalism. The Catholic key is balance: private property, yes, but subordinated to the universal destination of goods; economic freedom, yes, but regulated by justice; the market, yes, but not as an absolute idol.

"The work of caring for the home and raising children — does that count as work? Society neither pays nor values it."

Domestic work and caring for children is one of the most valuable forms of work there is, and the Church has always defended it. That the market does not pay for it does not mean it has no value. On the contrary: raising a child, maintaining a home, caring for a sick elderly relative — that is directly participating in God's creative work. Many women have been belittled for dedicating themselves to the home; the Church reminds them that their work is irreplaceable and that a person's value does not depend on their paycheck.

"How can I sanctify a job I hate or that seems useless?"

Not all work is gratifying. There are monotonous, exhausting jobs, apparently meaningless. But the value of Christian work does not depend on how "interesting" it is, but on the love with which it is done and to Whom it is offered. A prisoner breaking rocks can be sanctified breaking rocks if they offer it to God. An office worker filing papers can do it with love. That said, the Church also encourages seeking more humane working conditions and aspiring to jobs that develop the person. It is not a virtue to resign oneself to the inhuman.

"If I am an employer, how do I know if I pay a just wage?"

The Catechism gives criteria: the wage must be sufficient for the worker to support their family with dignity, to save something for the future, and to have a margin of security (CCC 2434). It is not "what the market pays." If your company makes profits but your employees can't make it to the end of the month, something is wrong. Social doctrine encourages employers to see their workers as collaborators, not as costs to be minimized. A good examination: would you live as your workers live on the wage you pay them?

"What if I have to work on Sunday? Is it a sin?"

The Sunday obligation is to keep the Lord's day holy, not merely to "do no work." If your work is necessary for the community (nurse, police officer, baker who makes the day's bread) and you cannot avoid working, you do not sin. But you should, as far as possible, find another time for Mass, rest, and prayer. The moral problem arises when one works on Sunday out of greed — to earn more money — or when an employer forces their workers to work on Sunday without genuine necessity.

Catechist's Prayer

Lord God, who in the Nazareth workshop
sanctified human work through the hands of your Son:
Grant me wisdom to show my students
that working well, with love, for you,
is already being in heaven while still on earth.
Help me to teach that no one is "just a worker,"
because every worker is your child, a temple of the Spirit,
called to holiness from their trade, their desk, their kitchen.
May young people discover in study a vocation,
adults in their employment an altar,
and the weary in Sunday rest a foretaste of your Kingdom.
Through the intercession of St. Joseph the Worker and the Virgin Mary,
teach us to do everything for love.
Amen.
